planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just at the soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; magic lily performs best when allowed to dry out after the spring foliage dies — do not water heavily in June–July as the bulbs are dormant.

directions: Plant bulbs with the neck at or near the soil surface in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun — magic lily earns its name by following a seemingly magical seasonal cycle: broad, strap-like leaves emerge in early spring, grow through April, then disappear entirely by June, leaving no trace for two months. Then, in July or August, apparently bare ground suddenly produces clusters of tall, leafless stalks topped with large (3–4 inch), fragrant, pink to lavender-pink lily-like flowers in a spectacular summer surprise that inspired the common names 'surprise lily' and 'resurrection lily.' The flowers last 1–2 weeks. Interplant with hostas, daylilies, or groundcovers to hide the disappearing spring foliage. Clumps increase slowly and bloom more prolifically when undisturbed — divide only every 7–10 years when overcrowded. One of the most reliably carefree bulbs for the southern garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just below the soil surface in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade; white spider lily follows the same leafless-flower cycle as red spider lily — flowers appear on bare stalks in late summer and fall, before the foliage emerges in winter.

directions: Plant bulbs with the neck at or near the soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — white spider lily is a rarer and more refined cousin of the familiar red spider lily (Lycoris radiata), producing pure white spider-like flowers with long, arching stamens and reflexed petals on bare 18–24 inch stalks in August–September, just as summer heat begins to break. The white flowers are luminous in evening light and especially effective planted in masse with red spider lilies for striking contrasting colonies. The winter strap-like foliage is attractive through spring, then dies back completely in summer. Like all Lycoris species, the bulbs contain toxic alkaloids and are deer-resistant; they naturalize reliably in the South with no supplemental irrigation once established. The elegant, ghostly white flowers create a dramatic late-summer effect when most other summer flowers have declined in heat.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; black haw is more drought tolerant than most viburnums and succeeds in drier, shadier conditions that defeat other viburnums.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — black haw is a native multi-stem large shrub or small tree of open woodlands, roadsides, and fence rows throughout the eastern US, valued for four seasons of ornament: flat-topped clusters of white flowers in April, lush summer foliage, brilliant scarlet to burgundy fall color, and clusters of ripening fruit that progress from green to pink to blue-black as fall advances. The dark blue-black berries are edible and have a sweet, raisin-like flavor when fully ripe, used traditionally to make preserves, wine, and fruit leather; they are also heavily consumed by migrating birds in fall. Black haw is one of the most drought-tolerant and shade-tolerant native viburnums — it thrives where other viburnums struggle — and brings exceptional wildlife value to naturalistic plantings and food forest understories.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix or transplant container specimens in spring; possum grape establishes rapidly in warm weather and is virtually indestructible once established — it tolerates extreme drought, deep shade, and root competition from large trees that kill most other vines.

directions: Root cuttings or plant in virtually any soil type — from dry, rocky, alkaline limestone to moist, sandy loam — in full shade to full sun — possum grape is one of the toughest native vines of the Gulf Coast and South Texas, a member of the grape family with succulent, deeply lobed, somewhat grape-like leaves on twining stems. The succulent stems store water, giving this vine exceptional drought tolerance even under large trees where root competition is intense and soil is bone dry. Small clusters of inconspicuous flowers in summer are followed by small, dark purple to black berries that are eagerly consumed by mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. It tolerates the deep shade under live oaks, cedar elms, and other large trees better than almost any other vine and is useful as a tough groundcover or fence cover for difficult, dry, shaded exposures. Deer avoid it. Deciduous in zone 7–8; semi-evergreen in zone 9–10.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in fall in well-drained, rocky to loamy, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas redbud is significantly more drought, heat, and alkaline-soil tolerant than the standard eastern redbud — it is the variety to choose for Gulf Coast and South Texas gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade — Texas redbud is the heat-and-drought-hardy western variety of our native eastern redbud, offering the same spectacular early spring bloom — dense clusters of magenta-pink pea-flowers covering every branch before the leaves emerge — with significantly improved performance in the rocky limestone soils, alkaline caliche, and intense summer heat of the western Gulf Coast and Edwards Plateau. The foliage is rounded, glossy, and notably thicker and more heat-tolerant than the standard species, holding up through brutal Texas summers without scorching. Selected cultivars like 'Oklahoma' (with very dark, glossy foliage and wine-red flowers) and 'Texas White' (clear white flowers) offer outstanding ornamental refinements over the species. An outstanding small patio or understory tree for dry, alkaline, sunny to lightly shaded garden sites in zone 6–9, requiring minimal water once established.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp bay grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, pond margins, swamps, and bottomlands of the Gulf Coast and Southeast — it requires consistently moist to wet soil and does not tolerate drought.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun — swamp bay is a native evergreen tree of Gulf Coast wetlands, closely related to the edible bay laurel and redbay, with aromatic leaves historically used as a bay leaf substitute in Creole and Southern cooking. The glossy, elliptic evergreen leaves have a whitish-downy underside and are fragrant when crushed with the characteristic bay laurel scent. Small, yellowish-green flowers in spring attract native bees; dark blue-black berries feed songbirds and the palamedes swallowtail butterfly uses the foliage as a larval host. It grows naturally in cypress swamps, wet flatwoods, and bay swamps throughout the Gulf Coast and is one of the best choices for naturalizing in wet, low areas where other trees refuse to grow. The wood is durable and aromatic; the tree is long-lived and forms the canopy of classic southern bay swamp ecosystems.

planting.note: Plant container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, alkaline to slightly acidic, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; blue hesper palm is cold-hardy to approximately 12–15°F when established and requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate wet or waterlogged soils.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — blue hesper palm is arguably the most ornamentally striking palm in the world for dry climates, producing enormous, rigid, deeply divided, silvery powder-blue fan fronds up to 6 feet across on a stout, solitary trunk that grows very slowly to 30–40 feet. Native to Baja California and northwest Mexico, it is one of the most cold-tolerant fan palms for dry-climate gardens, hardy to approximately 15°F once established, and thriving in the hot, dry conditions of zone 9–11 Southwest and Gulf Coast. The spectacular 15–20 foot long creamy-yellow flower stalks that extend dramatically beyond the crown are among the most dramatic flowering events of any palm. Extremely drought tolerant once established — planted in well-drained soil, it requires virtually no supplemental irrigation in warm, dry regions. Growth is very slow (6–12 inches per year), so purchase the largest specimen the budget allows.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container specimens in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; flatwoods plum grows naturally in the sandy, acidic, dry soils of pine flatwoods and woodland edges throughout the Gulf Coast and adapts well to similar garden conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade — flatwoods plum is a spectacular native small tree of the southeastern coastal plain and Gulf Coast pine flatwoods, producing masses of pure white five-petaled flowers on bare branches in February–March, among the earliest and most profuse of all native spring-blooming trees and a critical early nectar source for native bees before other flowers open. The small round fruits (3/4 inch) ripen in July to purple-black with a blue bloom; they are tart to sweet depending on the tree, used for preserves, jellies, wine, and as wildlife food for deer, foxes, and dozens of bird species. An excellent candidate for wildlife food plots and naturalistic plantings in the pine flatwoods region. Many individual trees have superior fruit; select superior-fruiting trees to propagate for orchard use. Drought tolerant once established in adapted sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile, loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; blue Louisiana iris tolerates slightly drier conditions than other Louisiana irises and blooms later in the season, making it valuable for extending the iris bloom season in Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — the zigzag iris is one of the parent species of the spectacular Louisiana iris hybrid group, producing large, rich blue-purple flowers on distinctively zigzag stems that hold the blooms close to the foliage in late spring. It is native to the wet bottomlands, swampy thickets, and stream margins of the Lower Mississippi Valley and Gulf Coast, where it grows in moister, shadier conditions than most other Louisiana iris species. The flowers have the classic Louisiana iris form — broad, spreading falls with a yellow signal patch and narrow, upright standards — in deep blue-violet that is stunning in mass plantings along pond edges and in rain gardens. It is one of the more cold-hardy Louisiana iris species, tolerating zone 6 winter cold well. An essential component of any wetland or rain garden featuring native iris species.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy to mucky soil in full sun to partial shade; giant blue iris naturally grows in salt-influenced freshwater and brackish marshes of coastal Louisiana and is uniquely tolerant of poor drainage, flooding, and coastal salt influence.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade — giant blue iris is one of the showiest of all Louisiana iris species and one of the primary parents of Louisiana iris hybrids, endemic to the coastal freshwater and brackish marshes, swampy forests, and wet prairies of coastal Louisiana. Its flowers are spectacularly large (5–7 inches across) in clear blue to blue-purple on tall stems (3–5 feet) that tower above the robust, broad leaf fans, blooming in April–May in spectacular drifts. It is significantly more salt and flood tolerant than other Louisiana irises, thriving in the challenging conditions of coastal garden areas with periodic saltwater influence. The species is an important wetland stabilizer in its native coastal marsh habitat. In gardens, it is outstanding for pond margins, rain garden edges, and any wet, low spot that holds water seasonally. Louisiana iris hybrids derived from this species have expanded greatly on its already exceptional ornamental traits.
